coredata.dc:title: Luminescent Eu3+/Gd3+ co-doped hydroxyapatite for selective sensing Cr3+ and Cu2+ ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Hongjuan
Mao, Yuanbing
coredata.dc:description: Both excess and deficiency of Cr3+ and Cu2+ ions can bring about certain abnormalities in human health, but it is still challenging to achieve great sensitivity and selectivity for sensing trace amount of copper and chromium ions due to the strong interference of coexisting ions in ambient water. To take advantage of excellent luminescence of rare earth (RE) ions and biodegradability and environmental friendliness of hydroxyapatite (HAP), novel luminescent Eu3+/Gd3+ co-doped hydroxyapatite (HAP/Eu-Gd) has been synthesized as a sensing material for the detection of Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions. Based on the fluorescence “turn-off” effect, Cr3+ and Cu2+ ions can be selectively recognized by their quenching fluorescence of the HAP/Eu-Gd. The HAP/Eu-Gd displays great sensitivity. Its limit of detection for Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions are 0.566 μM (35.967 μg/L) and 0.165 μM (8.579 μg/L), respectively, which are below the Cu2+ (2 mg/L) and Cr3+ (100 μg/L) contamination limits of drinking water stated from the United States Environmental Protection Agency. Moreover, the luminescence quenching mechanisms of the HAP/Eu-Gd for Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions were elucidated by X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectra, UV-Vis adsorption spectra, photoluminescence studies and luminescence lifetime measurements. Our study paves the way for RE-doped HAP luminescent sensors in potential applications for metal ion detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Luminescence
Eu3+/Gd3+ co-doped hydroxyapatite
Detection
Cr3+
Cu2+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions
Sensing properties of the HAP/Eu-Gd for Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions
Detection mechanisms of the HAP/Eu-Gd for Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: YM acknowledges the support by the IIT startup funds. HL acknowledges the China Scholarship Council (CSC201908430311) and the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2021JJ30563).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectrum, (b) TGA plots, (c) XRD pattern and Rietveld refinement, and (d) EDS spectrum of the HAP/Eu-Gd sample.
(a) PL excitation spectrum（λem = 616 nm) and (b) emission spectrum (λex = 394 nm) of the HAP/Eu-Gd sample.
(a) PL emission wavelengths and (b) luminescence intensity at 616 nm of Eu3+ from the HAP/Eu-Gd (2.5 mL) with Cu2+ and Cr3+ ion (100 µL, 0.01 M) and several other ions (100 µL, 0.01 M).

The concentration effect of (a) Cr3+ and (c) Cu2+ ions on the PL emission wavelength of the HAP/Eu-Gd at λ ex = 394 nm. The fitting curves of the PL emission peak intensity of the HAP/Eu-Gd at λ em = 616 nm with the (b) Cr3+ and (d) Cu2+ ion concentrations.

(a) FT-IR spectra of the HAP/Eu-Gd sensor before and after treated with Cr3+ and Cu2+. (b) XRD patterns of the HAP/Eu-Gd sample before and after treated with Cr3+ and Cu2+. (c) The PL emission spectrum of the HAP/Eu-Gd sample and the UV-Vis spectra of Cu(NO3)2·3 H2O and Cr(NO3)3·9 H2O water solutions. (d) PL lifetime spectra of the HAP/Eu-Gd sensor in the presence and absence of Cr3+ and Cu2+ with λ ex = 394 nm and λ em = 616 nm.
Illustration for the synthesis procedure of the HAP/Eu-Gd sensor and the recognition of Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions.
Comparison the LOD of the HAP/Eu-Gd with representative reported sensing materials for Cu3+ and Cr3+.

head.ce:title: Luminescent Eu3+/Gd3+ co-doped hydroxyapatite for selective sensing Cr3+ and Cu2+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Illinois Institute of Technology, 3101 South Dearborn Street, Chicago, IL 60616, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Illinois Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Illinois Institute of Technology, 3101 South Dearborn Street, Chicago, IL 60616, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Nuclear Science and Technology, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Nuclear Science and Technology, University of South China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Nuclear Science and Technology, University of South China, Hengyang, 421001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Both excess and deficiency of Cr3+ and Cu2+ ions can bring about certain abnormalities in human health, but it is still challenging to achieve great sensitivity and selectivity for sensing trace amount of copper and chromium ions due to the strong interference of coexisting ions in ambient water. To take advantage of excellent luminescence of rare earth (RE) ions and biodegradability and environmental friendliness of hydroxyapatite (HAP), novel luminescent Eu3+/Gd3+ co-doped hydroxyapatite (HAP/Eu-Gd) has been synthesized as a sensing material for the detection of Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions. Based on the fluorescence “turn-off” effect, Cr3+ and Cu2+ ions can be selectively recognized by their quenching fluorescence of the HAP/Eu-Gd. The HAP/Eu-Gd displays great sensitivity. Its limit of detection for Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions are 0.566 μM (35.967 μg/L) and 0.165 μM (8.579 μg/L), respectively, which are below the Cu2+ (2 mg/L) and Cr3+ (100 μg/L) contamination limits of drinking water stated from the United States Environmental Protection Agency. Moreover, the luminescence quenching mechanisms of the HAP/Eu-Gd for Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions were elucidated by X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectra, UV-Vis adsorption spectra, photoluminescence studies and luminescence lifetime measurements. Our study paves the way for RE-doped HAP luminescent sensors in potential applications for metal ion detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Both Cr3+ and Cu2+ ions are important trace elements in human nutrition. Either their excess or deficiency can bring about certain abnormalities in human health [1–3]. Particularly, the deficiency of Cr3+ may increase the risk of health disorders such as diabetes and cardiovascular diseases while the excess of Cr3+ may have negative impact on cellular structures [4]. Excessive Cu2+ in the body may cause gastrointestinal diseases, serious kidney or liver injury, and neurological disorders. Deficient Cu2+ may result in myelodysplastic syndrome and then cause severe anemia [5]. Hence, exploring efficient techniques to sense Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions have great significance. In general, common detection methods such as atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES), and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) can be very accurate in detecting low concentration levels of heavy metal ions. Nevertheless, these methods need costly and complex apparatuses. Luminescence detection using optical responses of phosphors is an attractive alternative analytical technique for metal ions owing to its fast response time, real-time, convenience and simplicity [6–12]. Luminescent sensors such as CdSe quantum dots [13], carbon dots[14,15], graphene quantum dots [16], CsPbBr3 perovskite quantum dots [17], and metal-organic frameworks (MOF) [18–20], have been prepared for sensing Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions. However, the preparation cost of MOF is high, and the stability of most MOF is still not ideal, especially in strong acid and alkali aqueous solution. CdSe quantum dots are toxic. Moreover, it is still challenging to achieve great sensitivity and selectivity for sensing trace copper and chromium due to the strong interference caused by various coexisting ions in ambient water. The development of economical, environmentally friendly, sensitive, and selective light-emitting sensors for the recognition of trace Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions still need to be strengthened.

Hydroxyapatite (HAP, Ca10(PO4)6(OH)2) is the dominating inorganic composition in human and animal hard tissues (bone and teeth) and is a low cost, biodegradable, and environmentally friendly material [21,22]. It can be used as the main host lattice of luminescent sensors with doped rare earth (RE) ions. Because RE ions have an ionic radius close to Ca2+, they can replace Ca2+ in HAP. RE elements have an unfilled 4 f electron shell, and the movement of 4 f electrons at energy levels produces unique luminescence properties. The emission spectra of rare earth ions have the advantages of excellent photo-stability, large Stoker shift, narrow and strong emission band, and long fluorescence lifetime, so RE ions are the ideal choice for the preparation of sensing materials with excellent luminescence functions [18,23–29]. Furthermore, HAP doped with RE ions has good luminescence characteristics and broad application prospects in biomedical field and metal ion detection [7,24,30,31]. The most luminescent materials based on HAP, which use RE as dopants such as Tb3+, Er3+, Eu3+, La3+ or Dy3+, have been used for biomedical applications. While there are a small number of RE doped HAP materials used in metal ion detection, and RE co-doped HAP for detecting Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions have never been reported. Most sensors for Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions are hindered by their low selectivity and sensitivity.

Herein, a novel luminescent Eu3+ and Gd3+ co-doped hydroxyapatite (HAP/Eu-Gd) sensing material for detecting Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions was prepared using agar as template through a facile biomimetic process ( Scheme 1). The HAP/Eu-Gd sensor was first applied to detect Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions with high selectivity and sensitivity based on its luminescence quenching effects. Moreover, we investigated the luminescence quenching mechanisms of the HAP/Eu-Gd for Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions by multiple analytical techniques. The mechanism of Cu2+ luminescence quenching involves the re-absorption of the emission light of the HAP/Eu-Gd and the occurrence of energy transfer from the HAP/Eu-Gd to Cu2+ ions. The mechanism of Cr3+ quenching involves the competitive absorption of excitation light, reabsorbing of the emission spectrum of the HAP/Eu-Gd, and the occurrence of energy transfer between the HAP/Eu-Gd and Cr3+ ions.

EuCl3·6 H2O, NaOH, Al(NO3)3·9 H2O, agar, and NaNO3 were received from Sigma Aldrich. Gd(NO3)3·6 H2O were provided by Acros Organics. NH4H2PO4 was received from Fluka. Ca(NO3)2·4 H2O was received from Fisher Scientific. Cr(NO3)3·9 H2O and Cu(NO3)2·3 H2O were obtained from Acros Organics and Fisher Scientific, respectively. Absolute ethanol La(NO3)3·6 H2O, Ce(NO3)3·6 H2O, KNO3, Sr(NO3)2, and Zn(NO3)2·6 H2O were provided by Alfa Aesar. (UO2)(NO3)2 were provided by Fluka and United Nuclear Corporation.

HAP/Eu-Gd was prepared in the following route: The dissolution of 0.0349 g Gd(NO3)3·6 H2O, 0.017 g EuCl3·6 H2O and 0.365 g Ca(NO3)2·4 H2O in 10 mL deionized water (DI water) was acquired Solution-A. Next, 0.02 g agar was added into 10 mL DI water and then heated to acquire Solution-B. Meanwhile, the dissolution of 0.115 g NH4H2PO4 in 10 mL DI water was obtained Solution-C, the pH of which was regulated to 10 with 1 M NaOH water solution. Afterwards, Solution-A was added dropwise into Solution-B with violent agitation, and then Solution-C was introduced dropwise to the Solution-A and Solution-B mixture. In the next step, the pH of mixed solution was regulated to 10–11 by 1 M NaOH water solution, and then the mixed solution was stirred at 50 ℃ and 400 r/min for 4 h. Finally, the precipitate obtained by centrifugation was washed and then dried in an oven (70 ℃) overnight.

FT-IR spectra were recorded with a Perkin Elmer from 400 to 4000 cm−1 Spectrum Two spectrometer. XRD patterns were taken from a Bruker D2 Phaser diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation in the 2θ range of 10°− 80° with a scan of 1° min−1. TGA plots were measured by a TGA instrument (Mettler Toledo) at the temperature from 25 ℃ to 800 ℃ in an air atmosphere with a heating rate (10° min−1). UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beckman Coulter DU 800) was used to measure UV–vis absorption spectra. The characterization of sample morphology was carried out on a SEM, and elements analysis was conducted with energy dispersion spectrum (EDS) (Phenom pro). Measurements of photoluminescence (PL) were performed through a FLS1000 spectrofluorometer with a Xe lamp as the excitation source. Luminescence lifetime measurements were performed on this spectrofluorometer FLS1000 with a pulsed Xe source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: To test the selectivity of ion detection, 0.001 M Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions (100 µL) and 0.01 M several other metal ions (10 µL) were introduced into the aqueous HAP/Eu-Gd emulsions (0.2 mg/mL, 2.5 mL) in quartz cuvettes, respectively. The other interfering metal ions for selective sensing test include different types of metal ions which commonly exist in water, including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Sr2+, La3+, Ce3+, Gd3+, Zn2+, and Al3+. For further investigating the sensing sensitivity of Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions, the as-prepared HAP/Eu-Gd emulsions (0.2 mg/mL, 2.5 mL) were mixed with either Cu2+ or Cr3+ containing solutions with various concentrations. After the HAP/Eu-Gd emulsions and metal ions mixed completely, PL intensity of the mixtures was measured with the FLS 1000 spectrofluorometer.

FT-IR spectrum of the HAP/Eu-Gd sample displays the peaks at 1028 cm−1, 562 cm−1 and 602 cm−1 ( Fig. 1a), which correspond to the vibrational modes of PO4 3- groups [32–34]. Also, two peaks at 870 cm−1 and 1412 cm−1 are attributable to the vibration of CO3 2-, demonstrating the substitution of CO3 2- for PO4 3- [35–37]. The peak located at 1480 cm−1 is associated with the C-H vibration from agar [30]. Moreover, the broad peak at approximately 3346 cm−1 and the peak at 1632 cm−1 are assigned to –OH stretching and the characteristic absorbed water H−O−H bending vibration, respectively [37,38].

TGA plot of the HAP/Eu-Gd (Fig. 1b) displays a weight loss of 30.2% at 25–100 °C, which could be attributable to the evaporation of surface adsorbed moisture. The weight loss of 12.5% at 100–500 °C could be related to the loss of absorbed water and some agar. Finally, the weight loss of 1.9% at 500–800 °C might be associated with the apatite crystals dehydroxylation.

As shown in Fig. 1c, the lattice constants of the HAP/Eu-Gd (a = 0.945 nm and c = 0.690 nm) derived from Rietveld refinement of XRD patterns is close to the lattice parameters of standard HAP (a = 0.937 nm and c = 0.688 nm) with a little increase [39]. This may be due to the substitution of Ca2+ ions by Eu3+ and Gd3+, resulting in the lattice distortion of HAP crystal and the change of unicellular parameters.

The SEM image of the HAP/Eu-Gd sample (Fig. S1) displays many particles with a fluffy appearance. Its corresponding EDS spectrum (Fig. 1d) indicates the presence of Eu, Gd, P, O and Ca elements, the atomic ratios of which are 2.93%, 5.12%, 20.66%, 45.76% and 25.53%. The above XRD, TGA, SEM, FT-IR and EDS characterization prove the successful synthesis of pure HAP/Eu-Gd sample.

The PL excitation wavelengths and emission wavelengths of the HAP/Eu-Gd sample are displayed in Fig. 2. The former (Fig. 2a) shows a maximum excitation wavelength at 394 nm, which can be related with the intraconfigurational 7F0 → 5L6 f–f transitions of Eu3+ ion with red emission. The emission wavelengths ( Fig. 2b) demonstrates four characteristic Eu3+ peaks at 491 nm, 616 nm, 650 nm, and 698 nm, which can be respectively associated with 5D0 → 7F1, 5D0 → 7F2, 5D0 → 7F3, and 5D0 → 7F3 transitions [25,40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing properties of the HAP/Eu-Gd for Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The luminescence changes of the HAP/Eu-Gd sample after adding different ions in water were investigated to explore its selective detection performance for metal ions. As seen in Fig. 3, the HAP/Eu-Gd emulsions showed an obvious luminescence quenching with the existing of Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions while they displayed slight luminescence intensity change with the existing of other ions such as La3+, Gd3+, Zn2+, K+, Sr2+, Ce3+, Al3+, Ca2+, and Na+.

Moreover, the luminescence intensity of the HAP/Eu-Gd sample reduces as the concentration of Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions increases ( Figs. 4a and 4c). The quenching efficiency of luminescence intensity were described from the Stern–Volmer equation as follow [41,42]:
I 0/I = 1 + K SV [Q]

Where I
0 and I are the original luminescence intensity of HAP/Eu-Gd and the luminescence intensity under the action of Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions, respectively. [Q] is the concentration of Cu2+ or Cr3+ ions. K SV is the luminescence quenching constant.

As seen from Fig. 4b and d, the plots of I 0/I vs. the concentration Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions display good linear relationships. The luminescence quenching constants (K SV) of the HAP/Eu-Gd are 1.31 × 105 M−1 and 1.23 × 105 M−1 for Cu2+ and Cr3+, respectively. According to the calculation in detail shown in Fig. S2 , the limit of detection (LOD) values of the HAP/Eu-Gd are 0.566 μM (35.967 μg/L) and 0.165 μM (8.579 μg/L) for Cu2+ and Cr3+, respectively, which are below the contamination limits of Cu2+ (2 mg/L) and Cr3+ (100 μg/L) in drinking water stated by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) [18,43]. The LOD values of the HAP/Eu-Gd for the Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions are less than most previous luminescent detectors as seen from Table 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanisms of the HAP/Eu-Gd for Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR, XRD, luminescence lifetime measurements, UV-Vis adsorption spectra, luminescence excitation and emission wavelengths were carried out to explore the mechanism of the HAP/Eu-Gd for detection of Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions. As seen from Fig. 5a, the FT-IR spectral intensity of all HAP/Eu-Gd peaks decreased after being treated with Cu2+ and Cr3+ aqueous solutions. Specifically, the peak at 3346 cm−1 corresponding to –OH group shifted to 3368 cm−1 and 3398 cm−1 after interacting with Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions, respectively. The peak of PO4 3- groups at 1028 cm−1 shifted to 1026 cm−1 and 1030 cm−1 after interacting with Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions, respectively. Moreover, the peak of PO4 3- groups at 870 cm−1 shifted to 874 cm−1 after being treated with Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions. The above shifts of peaks indicate the –OH and PO4 3− groups could be bonded with Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions.

XRD measurements were carried out to explore the potential structure changes of the HAP/Eu-Gd before and after being treated by Cu2+ and Cr3+ aqueous solutions. As seen from Fig. 5b, the XRD patterns of HAP/Eu-Gd after being treated by Cu2+ and Cr3+ aqueous solutions became weaker, which could be ascribed to the decreased crystallinity of the HAP/Eu-Gd caused the Cu2+ and Cr3+ treatment, but they still had characteristic peaks like those of the HAP/Eu-Gd. This observation indicates that the crystal structure of the HAP/Eu-Gd kept intact after the Cu2+ and Cr3+ aqueous solution treatment. Therefore, the luminescent quenching of the HAP/Eu-Gd was not brought about by the collapse of its crystal structure.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cr3+ ions in Fig. 5c showed that excitation light at 394 nm can be absorbed by Cr3+ ions. This result indicated Cr3+ can compete with the HAP/Eu-Gd for excitation light, which led to the luminescent quenching of the HAP/Eu-Gd by interacting with Cr3+ ions. While Cu2+ ions do not absorb excitation light at 394 nm, revealing that the competition with the HAP/Eu-Gd for the excitation light by Cu2+ ions did not happen. Furthermore, the overlap was observed between the PL emission spectra of the HAP/Eu-Gd and the UV–vis spectra of Cr3+ and Cu2+ ions, which indicated that the emitted light from the HAP/Eu-Gd could be re-absorbed, and thus causes the luminescence quenching of the HAP/Eu-Gd by interacting with Cr3+ and Cu2+ ions.

For deeper understanding of the detection mechanisms, the luminescence decay of the HAP/Eu-Gd was measured in the absence and presence of Cr3+ and Cu2+ ions. The decay curves (Fig. 5d) followed biexponential lifetime decay equations and the corresponding fitting parameters were displayed in Table S1. The average lifetime of the HAP/Eu-Gd is 604.52 µs while it decreased to 321.94 μs and 432.08 μs in the presence of Cr3+ and Cu2+, respectively (Fig. 5d and Table S1). The decreased lifetimes of the HAP/Eu-Gd after being treated with Cr3+ and Cu2+ ions demonstrated that energy transfer or electron transfer might take place from the HAP/Eu-Gd to Cr3+ and Cu2+, which contributed to rapid exciton decays and short lifetimes, thus causing the luminescent quenching. For the occurrence of energy transfer, there must be an overlap between the donor's emission spectrum and the acceptor's absorption spectrum [17]. As seen from Fig. 5c, a spectral overlap was found between the PL emission wavelength of the HAP/Eu-Gd and the UV-Vis absorption spectrum of Cu2+ and Cr3+, which revealed that energy transfer process happened and therefore brought about the luminescence quenching. On the contrary, quite small variation was observed for the average luminescence lifetime of the HAP/Eu-Gd with the other ions, such as Ca2+, K+, Sr2+, Ce3+, and Gd3+ (Table S2), indicating that no energy transfer or electron transfer happened between the HAP/Eu-Gd and these other metal ions, and hence there was almost no occurrence of luminescent quenching [18,52].

From the above results, the luminescent quenching of the HAP/Eu-Gd by Cu2+ can be attributed to the re-absorption of the PL emission from the HAP/Eu-Gd and the occurrence of energy transfer between the HAP/Eu-Gd and Cu2+ ions. On the other hand, the luminescent quenching of the HAP/Eu-Gd by interacting with Cr3+ ions may be ascribed to the competition absorption of excitation spectrum, re-absorption of the PL emission of the HAP/Eu-Gd along with the occurrence of energy transfer between the HAP/Eu-Gd and Cr3+ ions.

Our findings in this study exhibited the first example of a RE-doped HAP luminescent sensor with biodegradability and environmental friendliness for the selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+ and Cr3+ ions based on luminescence quenching effects. The sensing mechanisms of Cu2+ could involve the PL emission re-absorption from the HAP/Eu-Gd and the occurrence of energy transfer between the HAP/Eu-Gd and Cu2+ ions. On the other hand, Luminescence quenching by Cr3+ could involve the competitive absorption of excitation spectrum, the PL emission re-absorption from the HAP/Eu-Gd, and the occurrence of energy transfer between the HAP/Eu-Gd and Cr3+ ions. The detection limit of the HAP/Eu-Gd for Cu2+ (35.967 μg/L) and Cr3+ (8.579 μg/L) are much less than that of most reported sensors and the contamination limits of drinking water stated by USEPA, demonstrating it is a prospective luminescent detector. Our work delivers insight into developing luminescent RE-doped HAP-based sensors with high selectivity and sensitivity for the recognition of metal ions in aqueous environment.

Mao Yuanbing: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Liu Hongjuan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: YM acknowledges the support by the IIT startup funds. HL acknowledges the China Scholarship Council (CSC201908430311) and the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2021JJ30563).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.133413.

.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanocage-based N-rich metal–organic framework for luminescence sensing toward Fe3+ and Cu2+ Ions
Inorg. Chem.
